title: [Closes 15109] Add flag "--compression-format" to "podman manifest push" (and other fixes)
body:
Add flag "--compression-format" to "podman manifest push" both in local/remote mode. Fix usage of flag "--compression-format" for remote "podman image push". Fix usage of flags "--format", "--remove-signatures" in remote "podman manifest push".

Closes #15109.


#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
 - Added flag "--compression-format" to "podman manifest push"
 - Fixed usage of flag "--compression-format" for remote "podman image push".
 - Fixed usage of flags "--format" and "--remove-signatures" in remote "podman manifest push"
```

Note about the quality of this pull request: I definitely have no idea what I am doing, this is purely based on copy/pasting ;)

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *rhatdan*, *Romain-Geissler-1A* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

Hi,

I will wait tomorrow till a new image `quay.io/podman/upstream` is published and validate that now things are working for me with the different scenarios that used to fail (manifest push with a compression format, but also just a simple image push with a format (oci vs docker) or a compression format, which I tried these last days and was not working with a remote podman).

If it happens to work, how ok would it be to merge this in branch 4.2 ? I know, I am again very very late in the 4.2 releasing process, you have already frozen everything for the CentOS/RHEL release you are about to test these next couple of months, so this is not the kind of demand you usually like, but, still, I am asking ;)

Cheers,
Romain
New/unplanned features usually don't go in that late but I for sure empathize with the desire :)  Would be using `skopeo copy` an alternative for the time being?

For the time being, I modified our CI months ago to use `quay.io/podman/upstream` because as I implement new things on our side (build more and more things using podman remote rather than "local" podman, using manifest with multi-arch instead of pushing only x86_64 images, now using zstd:chunked, trying to fallback to OCI manifest/images as we have clients like "jib" which don't support OCI images), and regularly I find podman bugs that you always fix very quickly, but which in the end take 3 to 6 months to appear in a RHEL release. So it's been actually a long time our builds work only in the CI, and not locally when developers clone my repositories and just type `make` ;) I can survive like this some more time, I am used to it now. Yet, I will definitely never understand the Red Hat releasing policy, like I expressed it recently in your customer satisfaction feedbacks ;)

Thanks for the feedback, @Romain-Geissler-1A!  At times I find some policies strange as well as I see the needs from upstream such as in this thread.  The timing here is not ideal as we are already in the release-candidate phase.  That means we only test, stabilize and fix.  Getting new features in throws this process back a bit and, if done too often, would block releases.

(troll mode one) Well, at least you release more often than docker :D (troll mode off)

More seriously (and I promise to stop after this message, this issue is not the place to discuss this), I consider that projects are in good shape when they can afford to release often, like up to several times a week if needed. That of course has the nasty consequence of creating even more fragmentation in your users code base, but at least fixes are shipped quickly to the ones who need it. I took back several projects internally in Amadeus who used to have release cycle of several months, and each time the first thing I did was to fully automate the releasing, and "change the mindset (by force)" of my users (by forcing them to consume releases more and more often, till up to several releases a week). I break things now much much more often, but I also fix things much much quickly than before. I definitely understand you can't do that at Red Hat, because for 1 customer like me, you have 1 millions customer asking you to never change anything :D

No worries, I enjoy this conversation :)

A problem I see/experience with releases is that they come at cost.  Some things cannot be automated and require manual steps and review etc.  That means that the more we release, the less we get done.  Finding the right balance is delicate.

I think releasing to RHEL every 12 weeks is the best we can do.  Releasing up stream every couple of months new features with bug fix releases a little more often makes sense.

The other issue is with distributions.  Contributors that package up releases for other distros like Debian/Ubuntu ... could push back on more frequent releases.

As the one who usually handles releases, I am *wholeheartedly* in favor of more frequent drops. Release notes for quarterly releases make me want to tear my hair out, and while the automation we're developing will assist in that, the most surefire way to reduce the amount of time each release takes is to make them smaller and more frequent.

Looks like I have planted a seed, and you guys might one day discuss this at the coffee machine ;)

The way I see it, when you release often for sure not everyone picks/migrates to each and every new release. But the ones who want, at least they can.

I also considered several times for us to cut down testing phases by abandoning RHEL in favor of CentOS Streams, however when I started to throw away the idea to get the different feelings in Amadeus, each time the word "beta" came out, people were afraid (purely psychological, it wasn't backed by rational reasons/experiences). This idea was definitely burried for us when I asked Red Hat what would happen in case of a bug on a CentOS Stream package, and I was answered that I would be totally alone. It's like from a contract point of view RHEL and CentOS Stream are 120% different products, while in practice we all know it's like 90% identical, maybe even more.
